s1_b1: Two cars hit a wall at the same speed. One crumples slowly. One stops instantly.
s1_b2: Same speed in. Same dead stop. One driver walks away. One does not.
s1_b3: The motion that had to be killed was identical. So what made the difference?
s1_b4: Call that quantity of motion momentum — mass moving, how much there is to stop.
s1_b5: To change momentum you need a force. But force alone isn't the whole story.
s1_b6: A force acting for a long time, and a much bigger force acting for an instant, can do the very same job.
s1_b7: It's force multiplied by the time it acts. That product is the impulse.
s1_b8: And the impulse equals exactly the change in momentum. That's the whole bridge.
s1_b9: Now the crash makes sense. Same momentum to remove. The crumple zone stretches the stopping time —
s1_b10: — so the force needed drops. The rigid car stops in an instant, and the force spikes.
s1_b11: Airbags, knees bending on landing, a boxer rolling with a punch—all the same trick. Buy time, cut the force. Watch: the airbag stretches the stop from a tenth of a second to half a second—force drops fivefold.
s1_b12: Computing the impulse from a force acting over a time interval, and the change in momentum it produces — that's yours.
